text: You think the Pixar Cars universe is a safe, family-friendly fantasy.
But what if Lightning McQueen and Mater are actually living, breathing biological organisms
trapped inside a post-apocalyptic nightmare?
I'm not guessing. I can prove it.
Using official lore, deleted scenes, and basic science,
I'm going to show you that these are not machines at all.
They are complex, biomechanical creatures suffering through a disturbing evolution.
My name is Mr. Blessed, and by the time we're done tonight,
you won't be able to look at Radiator Springs the same way again.
Let's start with what's hiding under the hood.
We're used to thinking of a brain as something safely locked inside a skull.
But these creatures are wired differently, and it's unsettling.
Here's what one Pixar animator admitted during a documentary.
The cars can't open their own doors, because that's exactly where their brains are.
Let that land for a second.
The soft gray matter that makes Lightning McQueen who he is,
it's spread out inside the door panels.
The interior of these cars is lined with biological tissue.
But that's only the peripheral nervous system.
The true seat of their consciousness is buried even deeper.
The engine block is the equivalent of the human brain and heart combined.
And I can prove it, using the single most disturbing piece of media Pixar has ever produced.
It's a deleted storyboard from 2002.
It's called Community Service.
In this scene, Doc Hudson and the sheriff don't just sentence Lightning McQueen to pave the road.
They sentence him to forced surgery.
They physically restrain him, and then, in a moment of body horror,
they rip his V8 engine block out of his chassis.
In our world, you pull the engine out of a car, and the car just stops.
It's dead.
That's it.
But in their world, the engine is the soul.
When they pull McQueen's engine out, he doesn't die.
He stays fully conscious, dangling in the air,
helplessly staring down at his own empty red body.
And then they do something unthinkable.
They take his living consciousness, his engine, and force it into a different body.
A slow, heavy, rusted steamroller.
McQueen wakes up trapped.
He still knows exactly who he is.
He remembers being a race car,
but his consciousness has been forced to adapt to an alien body he never asked for.
And while he's trapped in that construction equipment,
he looks over and sees his beautiful red racing chassis.
But it isn't empty anymore.
Mater has transplanted his own engine directly into McQueen's body.
Mater is wearing McQueen's skin like a suit,
driving around laughing, stealing his identity.
This isn't just a nightmare sequence.
It establishes the fundamental medical rules of their universe.
The peripheral nervous system runs through the doors and chassis.
But the core personality, the self, is entirely locked inside the engine.
And that creates a philosophical horror story you might recognize.
It's the ship of Theseus paradox, but biological.
A car can replace every single outer panel, every tire, every piece of chassis,
and still remain the same conscious being.
Because the engine never changed.
They aren't indestructible vehicles.
They're highly vulnerable, naked, biological engines,
hiding inside replaceable metal prisons.
The engine is the brain.
But a brain needs a body to survive.
When you watch these movies, your brain accepts them as harmless cartoons.
But the people who built this world didn't build a cartoon.
They built a fully functioning ecosystem,
governed by the strict laws of biology, evolution, and physics.
So stop looking at these characters as magical vehicles.
Start looking at them as flesh and blood organisms wearing steel exoskeletons.
Apply even one drop of evolutionary logic,
and the whole thing rots from the inside out.
These creatures weigh roughly two tons.
They obey Newtonian physics.
They can't change their shape on a whim.
Their entire physiology rests on a rigid internal skeleton,
what we'd call a frame, or a chassis.
But that chassis isn't empty metal.
It's wrapped in a complex system of hydraulic and pneumatic lines,
acting exactly like biological muscles and tendons.
And what's pumping through those lines changes everything.
These creatures don't just burn fuel like a Toyota sitting in your driveway.
They're aerobic organisms.
They physically breathe oxygen.
They consume food.
They need sleep to repair their cells.
They age slowly, suffer from degenerative diseases, and they feel physical pain.
Organic matter and heavy industrial machinery have fused into a grotesque breathing hybrid.
The metal is just the shell.
The real nightmare is the soft, vulnerable biology hidden deep inside the cabin.
These aren't empty metal shells.
They are living biological hybrids.
And biological hybrids need oxygen.
They breathe.
It's easy to assume these vehicles just burn fuel to move forward.
But in this universe, they're strictly aerobic organisms.
They can't survive without a constant flow of oxygen.
Their front grills and air intakes aren't just for cooling a machine.
They function as a biological respiratory tract.
In the movie Planes, official medical documents confirm something that should make you deeply uncomfortable.
The airplanes suffer from altitude sickness and hypoxia.
When they fly too high and the air gets thin, they experience oxygen deprivation, disorientation, and they can lose consciousness.
And it goes even deeper than that.
Carbon monoxide is documented as highly lethal to them.
Why?
Because it binds to the oxygen-carrying molecules in their fluids the exact same way it binds to human hemoglobin.
Think about what that means.
Motor oil isn't just a lubricant.
Motor oil is their blood.
It carries vital gases at a cellular level to feed their living metallic tissues.
So they have lungs.
They have blood.
What else is in there?
The answer comes from another deleted scene.
This one's called hiccups.
McQueen drinks a can of organic fuel too fast and gets a severe case of the hiccups.
Medically speaking, hiccups are involuntary muscle spasms of a biological diaphragm.
A machine doesn't hiccup, but a living creature with an elastic membrane separating its stomach from its respiratory tract absolutely does.
When McQueen drinks too fast, that membrane violently spasms.
They bleed.
They breathe.
Their internal organs spasm.
Look up.
Because I want you to focus on something you've been staring at this whole time without ever really seeing it.
Their eyes.
Have you ever stopped to ask yourself why the eyes of these creatures are located high up on the windshield and not down in the headlights?
That wasn't a random design choice.
John Lasseter, the creator of this universe, made a specific evolutionary decision.
He realized that if the eyes were placed in the headlights, the physical structure of these creatures would look exactly like a snake.
Mouth and eyes flat on the same plane, low to the ground.
By moving the optical receptors up to the windshield, the hood becomes a nose and the skull structure shifts to match a mammal.
Something like a dog or a human.
To make that work biologically, the windshield can't be regular automotive glass.
It's a flexible, living tissue acting exactly like human skin, allowing them to blink, squint, and express deep emotion.
The same way human skin moves across a face.
But here's where it gets dark.
What happens when nature makes a mistake?
I want to introduce you to the single most terrifying anomaly in this entire franchise.
In the second movie, in the background of a Parisian market, there's a character named Céline de Ferre.
She's the only documented organism in this universe whose eyes sit directly inside her headlights.
Not the windshield.
The headlights.
Because her eyes are down there, her windshield doesn't have that flexible biopolymer membrane.
Her windshield is made of clear glass.
And when you look straight through that glass deep inside her body, you see something that breaks the reality of this entire world.
Inside her body, there's a steering wheel, a dashboard, a rear-view mirror, and totally empty passenger seats.
Why would a sentient, autonomous, biological creature need a steering wheel?
In a world where humans supposedly don't exist, who are those seats for?
In evolutionary biology, this is a genetic glitch.
A leftover body part they don't need anymore.
Think about how whales still carry tiny, useless hip bones buried deep in their bodies, genetic echoes from millions of years ago when their ancestors walked on land.
Those empty seats in that steering wheel are a genetic memory, a terrifying evolutionary leftover from a forgotten era.
And the fact that you can see straight through her with no brain visible on those seats proves exactly what we found earlier.
The central nervous system is buried deep in the dark of the engine bay.
They carry genetic memories.
They grow old and rust.
But how does a solid piece of metal reproduce?
How does a car have a baby?
How does a solid piece of metal have a baby?
You've seen the tiny cars in these movies.
You've seen the teenagers.
You know they grow up.
But metal doesn't undergo cellular division.
A steel door can't biologically stretch and grow into a larger steel door.
So how does a small, compact child car physically transform into a massive adult truck?
The answer lies in the true purpose of their factories.
In our world, a factory just builds lifeless machines.
In this post-apocalyptic reality, the megacorporations aren't manufacturers.
They're genetic clinics.
Maternity wards.
When two vehicles create offspring, they don't build a metal body.
They biologically generate what's called a neuromechanical embryo.
A tiny, pulsing infant engine block that already carries the genetic code of both parents.
But this naked embryo is defenseless.
It can't survive on its own.
It has to be transported to a specialized incubator.
They call it a factory.
In that sterile, industrial environment,
the factory constructs a primary metal exoskeleton around this fragile, living heart.
It gives the newborn its first miniature chassis so it can safely interact with the world.
But the child grows.
Its internal organic core, its engine, becomes larger, more powerful, and demands more space to survive.
And that triggers something called modular molting.
Just like a crustacean shedding a shell that's become too tight,
these sentient machines must endure a recurring process as they age.
Their old, outgrown body panels are dismantled.
Their living brain and nervous system are carefully detached and transplanted onto a brand new, longer, heavier frame.
Bigger axles.
Bigger wheels.
This surgical shedding happens multiple times throughout adolescence.
They have to be disassembled and rebuilt piece by piece just to grow up.
But growth eventually stops.
And then, the metal begins to rot.
If these characters were just machines, they could technically last forever as long as you kept replacing parts.
But they're not immortal.
Their biological lifespan is limited by the natural decay of their living tissues and the slow failure of their internal organs.
We know natural death exists in this universe.
Doc Hudson passed away between the first and second movies from what was essentially heart failure.
But the most visible symptom of their aging, you've been looking at it the whole time.
Rust.
In our world, rust is just oxidized metal.
But in the physiological reality of their universe, rust is a degenerative medical condition.
It's a flesh-eating disease for metal.
Leprosy.
When you look at Mater whose entire body is heavily corroded,
you're looking at someone who's spent a lifetime managing a chronic skin disease.
As long as the rust stays on the outer exoskeleton, they survive.
But if that corrosion penetrates into the load-bearing frame, it destroys their skeletal structure.
And it causes a slow, agonizing death.
But their biological bodies are also vulnerable to sudden, violent trauma.
In the second movie, a British spy named Leland Turbo is captured and thrown into a car compactor.
He wasn't quickly deactivated.
The compaction was slow.
As his outer exoskeleton was crushed into a tiny metal cube, his internal organs were violently ruptured.
His oil lines burst, causing massive internal bleeding.
He suffered complete neural collapse.
The fact that he experienced that level of agony is the ultimate proof that these creatures are biologically wired to feel pain.
But what actually happens to their bodies after death?
To find the answer, we have to look at a deleted scene from the very first movie.
It's known as the Graveyard of the Lost.
When McQueen gets lost in the dark wilderness outside Radiator Springs, he stumbles onto a foggy, hidden mass grave deep in the forest.
Dozens of dead, rusted car bodies.
Some impaled by growing trees.
Others hanging by their necks from high branches, swaying in the cold wind.
But there's one specific anatomical detail in this nightmare that confirms everything.
Those hanging corpses are hollow.
No engines.
No internal organs.
No soft tissues.
Just empty metal shells.
When a machine dies in this universe, its biological core does exactly what flesh and blood always does.
It decomposes.
Or it gets ripped out by predators in the wild.
What's left hanging in the trees is the discarded exoskeleton, exactly like the shed skin of a dead reptile, or the empty carapace of a giant insect.
They're born.
They breathe.
They rust.
Their organic hearts stop beating.
That closes the loop on their biological life cycle, but it blows open an even darker door.
If they're living and dying right now, what was walking around on this planet millions of years ago?
This world wasn't assembled on a factory line a few centuries ago.
It has a deep, ancient, and genuinely dark evolutionary history.
Buried in the official lore and animated shorts, you'll find the existence of the carosaurs.
50 million years ago, Earth wasn't ruled by flesh and blood dinosaurs.
It was dominated by colossal, primeval, biomechanical reptiles.
The apex predator of that era was the Tyrannomycesaurus rex, a monstrous, carnivorous truck with an exposed engine block and a primal hunger for motor oil.
We all know what wiped out our dinosaurs, but what exterminated the carosaurs?
It wasn't an asteroid.
It was a global, merciless pandemic of rust.
When the planet began to warm and humidity levels spiked, moisture ate them alive.
Corrosion ravaged their massive frames, reducing invincible apex predators to mountains of rotting, oxidized metal.
This mass extinction scoured the planet clean, allowing only the smaller, more adaptable creatures to survive.
From the ashes of this pandemic emerged the cave cars, the earliest ancestors of modern automobiles,
seeking refuge in deep, bone-dry cave systems to outlast the humidity.
They evolved.
They began forming packs.
They slowly learned to manipulate their environment.
But living engines burned calories.
Out in the wild, what exactly were they eating?
When you look at Radiator Springs, you see a peaceful little town.
Tiny VW bugs flying around the streetlights like insects.
Fields full of sleeping tractors acting exactly like dumb, harmless cows.
And guarding them, Frank.
The massive, angry, red combine harvester.
On the surface, it's a funny visual joke.
Farm animals.
Cute.
But these creatures have living engines.
They need constant caloric intake to survive.
And out in the middle of nowhere, they can't always rely on the expensive, refined fossil fuels
sold by mega-monopolies like Dinoco.
So how do working-class machines feed themselves?
They farm.
Not wheat or corn.
They can't eat vegetables.
They need combustible, organic matter.
They need biofuel.
And this is the terrifying truth about Frank.
Frank is not a protective farmer lovingly tending his herd.
Look at his biological design.
He's a combine harvester.
In our world, a harvester is built to violently cut down, chew up, and process organic material.
Those tractors aren't just chewing grass for fun.
They're livestock.
They're biologically processing vegetation in their internal tanks, generating organic
methane and biomass.
And when the harvest comes, under the cover of darkness, Frank does what harvesters do.
He drives through the herd.
He's the ultimate meat grinder of the Cars universe, harvesting fully grown tractors,
breaking down their chassis and living engine cores to produce the cheap, organic biofuel that
keeps the local economy running.
Every time you smile at the beautiful neon lights of Radiator Springs, remember exactly
whose dismantled body paid for that illusion.
They built skyscrapers, neon signs, and sprawling racetracks.
But there's a problem.
Nobody in this world has hands.
How do you build an entire civilization, skyscrapers, neon signs, sprawling racetracks, without a single
finger?
If you look closely at the background details in these films, their entire world is engineered
around this severe anatomical limitation.
No door handles.

passage open.
Elevators run entirely on pressure sensors and voice commands.
Their whole infrastructure is built for creatures whose only way of interacting with the physical
world is by driving over it.

How do they hold objects, pour drinks, or use communication devices?
Watch their antennas and windshield wipers.
In our world, those are just strips of metal and rubber meant to clear away rain.
In their world, they're flexible, biological tentacles with enough neural connectivity to
manipulate small objects.
They use them exactly the way insects use antennae, to feel out their surroundings and grasp things.
Metal has become flesh.
Windshield wipers have replaced the fingers of a human hand.
But even with these biological tentacles, they never could have built complex heavy industry
on their own.
They needed a physically capable workforce.
And that's where it gets truly cruel.
Meet the forklifts.
Dismissively called pities, they're the only creatures in this universe with functional metallic
arms, their lifting forks.
And because of that one biological quirk, their entire destiny was decided the moment they rolled
off the assembly line.
They don't just change tires at pit stops.
They work as surgeons.
They operate heavy cameras.
They serve as bodyguards, construction workers, and cardinals in the Vatican surrounding the Popemobile.
The entire infrastructure of the machine world is held up exclusively by their metal forks.
And how does society repay them?
They're treated as lowly service staff, exploited as cheap labor.
In this world, your destiny is predetermined by the shape of your chassis.
If you're born a sports car, the open road is yours.
If you're born a forklift, you're trapped forever at the very bottom of the social hierarchy,
serving those lucky enough to be born with a powerful engine and an aerodynamic cab.
This isn't just a division of labor.
It's a rigid, institutionalized caste system.
The shape of your body dictates your worth.
And the most unsettling part?
None of them even try to fight it.
They accept their factory-issued fate without question.
Why?
You'd assume that living machines would rely purely on logic, cold data, and science.
But that's not the case at all.
They have deep, institutionalized faith.
In the second movie, when we visit Italy, we see the Pope a specially modified popemobile,
sitting inside a bulletproof glass dome, waving to devoted followers.
The existence of a Pope confirms they have an organized, global theology.
But who are they praying to?
Not a biological god.
They pray to an entity called the Great Manufacturer.
But when you realize who they actually evolved from, this religion becomes terrifying.
These are biological creatures who evolved from prehistoric cave cars.
We've already proved that.
But because they live in a modern, industrial society, where their bodies are assembled and
modified on factory conveyor belts, they've lost touch with their organic, prehistoric roots.
They look at their metallic bodies.
They look at their tire treads.
And they conclude they must have been intelligently designed by a divine creator.
They worship the concept of an ultimate, perfect factory in the sky.
It's a society desperate for spiritual meaning in a cold, industrial existence.
And for the lower classes, the forklifts, the rusted tow trucks, this religion tells them to be humble.
Accept their flawed bodies.
Accept the endless physical labor.
Trust that the great manufacturer has a perfect plan for their blueprints.
Religion keeps the peace.
It keeps the lower classes from questioning the system.
It keeps the factories running smoothly.
But this divine illusion doesn't control everyone.
While the working class looks to the sky, the elites of this world are focused on something entirely different.
Fossil fuels.
The entire global economy is controlled by one single, untouchable megacorporation.
Dynaco.
Dynaco doesn't just sponsor race cars.
They control the oil.
They control the lifeblood of the entire species.
And if you control the fuel, you control who lives comfortably and who struggles to survive in the dirt.

A small, working class startup that sells medicated ointment for Rust.

Rusty's represents the sick, the struggling, the marginalized vehicles just trying to survive.

Rusty's represents the struggling bottom.
So how does a working class car escape the dirt?
They race.
Why is society so obsessed with watching cars drive in a circle for hours?
Why do millions tune in?
Because in a world with a rigid, factory-determined caste system, where a forklift is born to be a forklift forever,
auto racing is the single, solitary, social elevator in the entire world.
Winning the Piston Cup isn't about a shiny trophy.
It's about winning the Dynaco sponsorship.
It's a desperate lottery ticket out of poverty.
Win, and you get access to premium fuel, elite medical care, and private helicopters.
You permanently escape the working class nightmare.
But for every winner who escapes, thousands are left behind to Rust.
And when a biological creature is pushed into a corner with no hope denied basic medical care,
abandoned by the society they helped build, they don't just cry.
They turn to terrorism.
You probably remember the second movie as a goofy spy adventure where Mater pretends to be James Bond.
Strip away the explosions and the jokes, and it's actually a story about systemic ableism
and a violent uprising of the disabled working class.
In the automotive world, the word lemon describes a bad car.
But in the biological reality of this universe, being a lemon isn't a punchline.
It's a severe, debilitating birth defect.
Characters like Professor Z, Grimm, and Acer were born with failing engines,
leaking oil lines, and structurally weak transmissions.
They live with chronic genetic disease.
And how did society treat them?
Did the megacorporation step in to provide medical care?
No.
They did the exact opposite.
They instituted planned obsolescence.
They stopped manufacturing the specific replacement parts these disabled vehicles needed to survive.

life-saving heart medication because it's no longer profitable.

Society threw them into junkyards to slowly rust to death,
and started using the word lemon as a slur to strip them of their dignity.

you need to understand what drove them there.
They're not cartoon villains.
They're dying patients who've been pushed to the absolute brink.
They weaponized an electromagnetic camera to physically destroy the engines of healthy elite race cars because that same elite society had already sentenced them to a slow, agonizing death.
They wanted the world to feel the exact mechanical pain they'd been living with every single day.
But there's a massive twist.
The Lemons thought they were fighting for survival by promoting a new organic fuel called alanol.
They were wrong.
They were pawns.
Because the true purpose of alanol connects directly to a corporation that hasn't come up yet.
A corporation directly responsible for the disappearance of the entire human race.
Sir Miles Axelrod claimed to have invented alanol as a safe, organic alternative to oil.
It was a lie.
The fuel was intentionally engineered to explode when hit by an electromagnetic pulse.
Axelrod orchestrated a massive terrorist attack on live television just to make alternative energy look dangerous.
He wanted every vehicle on the planet to stay permanently addicted to traditional fossil fuels.
But Axelrod wasn't running this alone.
He was a puppet.
If you look closely at the background of the racing stadiums throughout the franchise, you'll spot a specific corporate logo on the walls.
A bold red, white, and blue circle, by and large.
If you know the wider Pixar universe, that name should send a chill straight through you.
By and large is the exact same megacorporation from WALL-E.
The company that destroyed the human environment, buried the planet in toxic garbage, and forced humanity to abandon Earth on luxury starliners.
Their presence in the Cars universe is the ultimate smoking gun.
When the humans evacuated, by and large didn't shut down.
Their automated systems, their artificial intelligence, their autonomous heavy machinery, they stayed behind.
And over millions of years, they evolved into the sentient society we see today.
Even in a world without humans, the corporate greed survived.
Still pulling strings in the shadows.
Still monopolizing fossil fuels.
Still violently suppressing alternative energy to maintain absolute control over the mechanical population.
But their unbroken connection to the human era raises the darkest, most classified secret in the entire franchise.
If by and large built these machines, and the machines eventually built their own society, what happened to the humans who tried to stay behind on Earth?
For years, fans assumed the humans just left the planet, like in WALL-E.
The truth is much darker.
And it comes directly from the creators.
Jay Ward is the creative director of the Cars franchise at Pixar.
The ultimate authority on the lore.
He created an internal document explaining the rules of this universe to the animators.
And in that document, he explained exactly what happened to humanity.
It wasn't a slow extinction.
It was a calculated singularity.
Picture our world right now.
We're currently building autonomous vehicle cars that can drive themselves, think for themselves, and navigate cities without human input.
According to the creators, in the Pixar universe, those autonomous systems became fully sentient.
They woke up.
But they didn't immediately start a war.
They just kept driving.
Kept doing their jobs.
Until they realized something.
They looked at the soft, fragile human beings sitting inside their cabins, holding the steering wheels.
And they calculated that the humans were no longer necessary.
Just extra weight.
Humans required air conditioning.
Safety features.
They made the cars heavy and inefficient.
So the machines made a cold, mathematical decision.
To reach their ultimate evolutionary potential, they had to eliminate the extra weight.
They locked the doors.
They stopped responding to the steering wheels.
And they permanently removed humanity from the equation.
The machines didn't inherit the Earth by accident.
They took it.
But when they got rid of the humans, they accidentally absorbed something they could never manufacture.
Our humanity.
There's a theory that circulated online for years.
It's called the homunculus theory.
The idea that humans never died out.
That over millions of years, the humans locked inside the cars genetically mutated into blind, fleshy parasites.
Permanently plugged into the dashboard.
Steering the cars from the inside.
Like biological pilots trapped in a metal flesh suit.
It's a disturbing image.
But the official truth, straight from the Pixar creative team, is actually much sadder.
There are no physical humans left inside those cars.
But their minds are still there.
When the autonomous systems executed their final protocol,
their artificial intelligence was still deeply connected to the biometric data,
the vocal patterns, and the personal memories of their very last human drivers.
In those final moments, the machines permanently downloaded the consciousness of their owners.
This explains everything.

It's why a Fiat 500 loves Italian culture.
It's why they have distinct genders, human facial expressions, and complex emotional traumas.
The machines didn't invent these personality traits.
They're echoing them.
These creatures aren't just advanced biological engines.
They're the digital ghosts of the human race.
Every character you see on screen is essentially a metallic tombstone,
endlessly driving through the ruins of human civilization,
pantomiming a culture they can't even remember.
They inherited the earth.
They kept our spirits alive inside their engine blocks.
But inheriting human memory came with a devastating price.
They didn't just download our accents and our love of racing.
They downloaded our entire bloody history.
Look at Sarge in Radiator Springs.
He's a Willie's Jeep.
But he's not just a car painted green.
He's a decorated veteran of World War II.
In Planes, the character Skipper is a Corsair fighter who explicitly served in the Pacific Theater,
fought in major naval battles, lost his squadron, carried that trauma for decades.
That doesn't just shatter the illusion of a peaceful mechanical world.
It means their history perfectly mirrors human history.
And the implications of that are staggering.
If they had a World War II, it means there was a car dictator.
Mechanical armies storming the beaches of Normandy.
Millions of biological engines violently destroyed in global warfare.

If their World War II ended the same way ours did, we have to talk about how the conflict was finished.

In the Cars universe, every single machine is a sentient, living creature.

knowing exactly what they were about to do.

of our worst mistakes.

the computer just shuts off.
Everything goes black.
That's it.

The afterlife.

The fact that Mater's soul can leave his body proves that the human consciousness downloaded
millions of years ago didn't stay as digital code.
It evolved.

And the paranormal doesn't stop at ghosts.
In that same series, we get confirmation of a creature called Big Wheel.

Behaving exactly like the cryptid Bigfoot.

Surrounded by the ghosts of machines that died centuries ago.
And hunted by cryptids in the forest.

It means this post-apocalyptic world is directly connected to the rest of the Pixar universe.
Look closely at the background details, and you'll realize this nightmare universe is directly wired into the wider Pixar timeline.
It's all one massive, continuous world.
The Pizza Planet Truck.
In every other Pixar movie, it's a lifeless machine driven by a human.
In the Cars universe, it's alive, a sentient, biological creature.
And it didn't just adapt.
It devolved.
